content.text: Structured data extraction from scraped HTML

is the process of turning raw, messy page markup returned by Playwright or Puppeteer into clean, schema-conformant JSON (or CSV/table) that Claude can reason over reliably. It is the critical "last mile" step between browser automation and agent intelligence — without it, Claude receives a wall of HTML tags, scripts, and noise rather than actionable data.

Think of the pipeline as a funnel: Playwright or Puppeteer captures the full rendered page (wide end), a parsing/cleaning step strips everything irrelevant (narrowing), and then either DOM selectors or Claude itself extracts only the fields you care about (narrow end). Claude's role is to act as the intelligent schema mapper, not as a raw HTML processor.
There are two distinct approaches in the Playwright/Puppeteer + Claude pattern, and they complement each other rather than compete.
You write explicit selectors in Playwright or Puppeteer code to extract known fields. Playwright and Puppeteer expose several extraction primitives:
Multiple elements of same type (all products in a list)
When CSS selectors are fragile or deeply nested
Intercept underlying XHR/fetch API calls for clean JSON data directly
The output of this step should be a structured object, not raw HTML:

When the HTML structure is unpredictable, varies per page, or you cannot maintain fragile selectors, you use Claude as the extraction engine. Playwright fetches and renders the page, your tool pre-processes the HTML, and then Claude maps the clean content to a schema.

This is the key innovation in the Playwright/Puppeteer + Claude pattern: Claude reads semantically cleaned text and returns schema-conformant JSON, eliminating brittle selectors that break on any site change.
Both strategies share a common pipeline structure:
Stage 1: Render with Playwright or Puppeteer
Wait for JavaScript to finish, interactivity to resolve, and the full DOM state to settle before extracting:
Stage 2: Clean and Reduce the HTML
The full rendered HTML is far too large and noisy to pass to Claude. Strip scripts, styles, navigation, footers, ads, and whitespace. Keep only the semantic content relevant to the extraction goal:

# Python - BeautifulSoup pre-cleaning before LLM
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

soup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'html.parser')

# Remove noise
for tag in soup(['script', 'style', 'nav', 'footer', 'iframe', 'noscript']):
    tag.decompose()

# Extract clean text with structure preserved
clean_text = soup.get_text(separator='\n', strip=True)

# Hard-limit to avoid token overflow
clean_text = clean_text[:8000]
Alternatively, target only the relevant HTML region rather than the whole page:

// Playwright - get only the relevant section's HTML
const sectionHTML = await page.$eval('#product-details', el => el.innerHTML);

Whether you use deterministic selectors or Claude extraction, define the output schema explicitly. With Claude's tool use, this becomes the tool's return type, and strict mode enforces it. With Python, Pydantic is the standard approach:

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional, List

class ProductData(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: float
    sku: Optional[str] = None
    in_stock: bool
    features: List[str] = []
    description: str
When passing to Claude, the schema definition drives the extraction prompt:

Extract product data from this page content and return it as valid JSON
matching exactly this schema:
  - name: string (product title)
  - price: number (USD, no currency symbol)
  - sku: string or null
  - in_stock: boolean
  - features: array of strings
  - description: string

Page content:
{clean_text}
Never pass Claude's raw extraction output directly to downstream systems. Validate against the schema, coerce types, and normalize values before returning the tool result:
Poor — "in stock" buried in class names
Excellent — Claude infers from natural language
In practice, a hybrid works best: use DOM selectors for fields that are always present in predictable locations (price, URL, image
), and use Claude for semantic or ambiguous fields (description quality, feature lists, sentiment, category classification).

One often-overlooked technique: many modern SPAs and e-commerce sites load data via internal JSON APIs before rendering. Playwright can intercept those network requests and extract the raw JSON directly — bypassing HTML parsing entirely:
This is by far the cleanest extraction method when available. Claude only needs to normalize and validate the result, not parse messy HTML.
Extracting full HTML and passing it to Claude
— context windows are finite, and full HTML is mostly noise. Always pre-clean and reduce before LLM calls.
alone is not enough on JS-heavy SPAs. Always wait for a meaningful DOM state.
— Claude's extraction may be slightly misformatted or have type inconsistencies. Validate every output before downstream use.
Using only text content, losing structure
loses DOM hierarchy signals that help Claude infer relationships between fields. Preserve some structural markers (newlines, headers) when cleaning.
— selectors should live in tool implementation, not in prompts, so Claude cannot be tricked via prompt injection into using adversarial selectors.
— remove scripts, styles, navigation, boilerplate before any extraction step.
Return compact JSON from the tool
— never return raw HTML to Claude; the tool should always return structured data.
Define schemas with Pydantic or JSON Schema
— gives Claude a precise extraction target and gives your application a validation contract.
Combine network interception with DOM fallback
— intercept API responses where available, fall back to DOM extraction when responses are unavailable or obscured.
Log extracted fields with the source selector or page region
— enables debugging when extractions drift over time.

Structured extraction is where the Playwright/Puppeteer + Claude combination delivers its greatest value: Playwright handles the mechanical work of rendering and DOM access, and Claude handles the semantic work of mapping ambiguous, variable-structure content onto a consistent, usable schema.
